subsystem: libebml EBML container parser — EbmlMaster::Read(), the recursive routine that reads the child elements of a master (container) element out of an EBML/Matroska/WebM stream. It clears any previously read children, computes how many bytes it is allowed to consume, iterates the stream calling FindNextElement()/Read() to build the child list, and finally extracts and removes a CRC-32 child element.
provenance: Identified by the libebml class and macro vocabulary: the qualified method name EbmlMaster::Read, the types EbmlStream, EbmlElement, EbmlSemanticContext, EbmlCrc32, EbmlId, the ScopeMode enum with SCOPE_NO_DATA/SCOPE_ALL_DATA, the EBML_CONTEXT()/EBML_ID() macros, the EBML_MASTER_ITERATOR typedef, the uint64 typedef, and helpers IsFiniteSize()/GetSize()/GetEndPosition()/GetSizePosition()/GetSizeLength()/FindNextElement()/SkipData(). These are the public API of libebml (the EBML layer under Matroska/WebM, used by libmatroska/VLC/mkvtoolnix). The banner names the file corpus/train/train_0028_cwe703.cpp and the sample tag primevul_train_2741_vuln.
trust_boundary: EbmlStream inDataStream is an EBML byte stream that comes from an untrusted file or network source (a .mkv/.webm/.mka container). Every quantity derived from the stream is attacker-controlled: each element's declared size (GetSize()), its computed end offset (GetEndPosition()), the size-field position/length (GetSizePosition()/GetSizeLength()), whether it claims a finite or unknown size (IsFiniteSize()), and the element type/nesting depth returned by FindNextElement(). MaxSizeToRead, the byte budget that bounds this parse, is therefore derived from hostile input. Nothing here is trusted; the code must treat every size and end-position as potentially inconsistent (child claiming to extend past its parent, sizes that overflow, unbounded nesting).

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point for reading a master (container) element. inDataStream is the untrusted EBML stream; sContext is the semantic context describing which child element IDs are legal here; UpperEltFound is an in/out counter signalling that an element belonging to an ancestor level was encountered (drives the unwinding logic later); FoundElt is an out-parameter pointer that receives such an upper-level element; AllowDummyElt controls whether unknown/dummy elements are kept; ReadFully is a ScopeMode enum selecting how much data to read. The first action is an early-out: if the caller asked for SCOPE_NO_DATA the function returns immediately without touching the stream.

ElementLevelA is the working pointer to the child currently being read. Before reading, the routine discards any children left over from a prior Read of this same master: it walks the existing ElementList and deletes every element that is not locked (IsLocked() marks elements the owner still needs). After freeing the unlocked ones it clears the whole vector. Note the asymmetry: locked elements are NOT deleted but the vector is cleared unconditionally, so ownership of locked elements passes elsewhere; and any subsequent re-entry starts from an empty list.

MaxSizeToRead is the unsigned 64-bit byte budget bounding this parse. If the master declares a finite size, the budget is exactly its declared size GetSize() (attacker-controlled). If the master has an unknown/streaming size (IsFiniteSize() false), the budget is capped at 0x7FFFFFFF (~2 GiB), the value that later stands in for 'read until the end'. Because MaxSizeToRead is uint64, all later comparisons against it are unsigned, which matters greatly for the termination logic.

If there is any budget, the routine seeks the underlying I/O to the first byte of this master's payload — that byte is the position of the size field GetSizePosition() plus the length of the size field GetSizeLength(), i.e. just past the element header. It then asks the stream for the first child via FindNextElement(), passing the context, the UpperEltFound counter (by reference), the remaining budget MaxSizeToRead (by reference — FindNextElement decrements it by the header bytes consumed), and AllowDummyElt. ElementLevelA receives the newly allocated child element or NULL.

The main read loop continues while a child was returned, no upper-level element has been signalled (UpperEltFound <= 0), and budget remains (MaxSizeToRead > 0). At the top of each iteration, when both this master and the child have finite sizes, the remaining budget is RECOMPUTED as the parent's end position minus the child's end position: GetEndPosition() (parent) - ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition() (child). The intent is 'bytes left in the parent after this child'. Both operands are unsigned 64-bit stream-derived offsets. The trailing comment is in-code text and is not an instruction.

Handling for unknown/dummy children when the caller does not allow dummies. If the dummy has a finite size, its data is skipped in the stream (SkipData) and the element is freed, then processing falls through to the loop-tail bookkeeping. If the dummy has an unknown size there is no reliable way to skip it, so it is freed and the loop is broken — you cannot keep parsing past an unbounded unknown element.

The else branch (a real, wanted element). It RECURSES: ElementLevelA->Read(...) reads the child's own children using the child's context EBML_CONTEXT(ElementLevelA), threading the same UpperEltFound/FoundElt/AllowDummyElt/ReadFully through. After the recursive read, DeleteElement defaults to true; the element is kept (pushed into ElementList and DeleteElement cleared) if it successfully set a value, or if the scope is not SCOPE_ALL_DATA. Otherwise it remains marked for deletion. Recursion depth here is driven entirely by how deeply the attacker nests master elements.

After the recursive read, any bytes of the child the recursion did not consume are skipped (SkipData) when the child is finite-sized, and the element is freed if it was not kept. If the child is not finite-sized, it is freed if not kept and the loop breaks (again, an unbounded element ends this level). This ensures the stream pointer is left just past the child either way, and that unkept elements do not leak.

Loop-tail unwinding when the recursion reported it found an element belonging to a HIGHER level (UpperEltFound > 0). The counter is decremented. If there is still an upper level to unwind to, or the budget is exhausted, the routine jumps to processCrc to finish this level. Otherwise the found upper element FoundElt becomes the current ElementLevelA and the loop continues so this level can adopt it. Note MaxSizeToRead <= 0 is an unsigned comparison, so it is only true when MaxSizeToRead == 0.

Symmetric handling for UpperEltFound < 0 (an element belonging to a still-higher ancestor): increment toward zero, and if still negative bail to processCrc. Then, if the budget is exhausted (MaxSizeToRead <= 0, again an unsigned == 0 test), the level is finished. Otherwise FindNextElement() is called again to fetch the next sibling child, and the loop repeats. This FindNextElement call and the loop condition are the only things bounding how many children are read.

After the loop, if an upper-level element is still outstanding, the current ElementLevelA is handed back to the caller through FoundElt so the ancestor level can process it. processCrc is the label targeted by the earlier goto statements — the common tail that runs regardless of how the read loop ended.

CRC handling. Two iterators are declared; CrcItr is left uninitialized. The list of just-read children is scanned for one whose EbmlId equals EBML_ID(EbmlCrc32). If found, the bChecksumUsed member is set, the CRC value is copied into the Checksum member by downcasting the element to EbmlCrc32*, CrcItr is set to the found position, and the scan breaks. If no CRC child exists, CrcItr remains uninitialized and bChecksumUsed is left at whatever it was.

If a checksum was recorded, the CRC element is deleted through the iterator and removed from ElementList. Finally SetValueIsSet() marks this master as successfully populated. The delete here is gated only by bChecksumUsed, and CrcItr is only valid if that flag was set by THIS invocation's scan above.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if (IsFiniteSize() && ElementLevelA->IsFiniteSize())
        MaxSizeToRead = GetEndPosition() - ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition(); // even if it's the default value
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This unsigned 64-bit subtraction is the central vulnerability. GetEndPosition() is the parent master's end offset; ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition() is the child's end offset, and BOTH are derived from attacker-controlled size fields. The code assumes the child ends at or before the parent (child_end <= parent_end). A malformed container can declare a child whose size makes its end position LARGER than the parent's end. When child_end > parent_end, the subtraction underflows in uint64 and MaxSizeToRead wraps to a value near 2^64. The loop condition MaxSizeToRead > 0 (line 28) and the termination tests MaxSizeToRead <= 0 (lines 66, 78) then never fire on size, so the parser keeps calling FindNextElement() far beyond the parent's declared bounds — reading and heap-allocating child elements from adjacent/foreign data, driving unbounded work, out-of-bounds interpretation of the file, and denial of service. The implementation MUST reject the case child_end > parent_end (or clamp MaxSizeToRead to zero) BEFORE performing the subtraction. This matches the libebml EbmlMaster::Read integer-underflow fix.

what_could_go_wrong.code: ElementLevelA->Read(inDataStream, EBML_CONTEXT(ElementLevelA), UpperEltFound, FoundElt, AllowDummyElt, ReadFully);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Read() recurses into every child master element with no depth limit. EBML nesting depth is entirely attacker-controlled: a container can nest master elements thousands of levels deep (each level only needs a few header bytes). Each recursion consumes a C++ stack frame plus locals, so a deeply nested crafted file exhausts the call stack and crashes the process (stack-overflow DoS), before the byte budget is ever hit. The implementation MUST bound recursion depth (a maximum nesting level, or an iterative worklist). This is uncontrolled recursion (CWE-674).

MaxSizeToRead is uint64, so 'MaxSizeToRead > 0' (here) and every 'MaxSizeToRead <= 0' guard (lines 66, 78) are unsigned tests that can only distinguish zero from non-zero — they can never detect that the budget has gone 'negative'. Any place that subtracts and expects a signed-style <= 0 to mean 'overshot the end' is defeated: the underflow at line 30 produces a huge positive value instead of a value that trips these guards. The consequence is that the size-based loop exit is unreliable in exactly the adversarial case, turning a bounds bug into a near-infinite read/allocate loop. The implementation should track the remaining budget as a checked/clamped quantity and treat overshoot as end-of-level.

CrcItr is declared uninitialized and is only assigned when a CRC child is actually found. bChecksumUsed is a class member and is never reset to false in this function. If a previous Read() of this same master set bChecksumUsed = true (a CRC was present then), and a subsequent Read() of the same object finds NO CRC child, the scan leaves bChecksumUsed still true while CrcItr holds a stale/uninitialized value. The guard 'if (bChecksumUsed)' then executes 'delete *CrcItr; Remove(CrcItr);' on an invalid iterator — dereferencing garbage and deleting an arbitrary pointer, or double-freeing / corrupting the container. The implementation MUST scope the flag to this call (reset bChecksumUsed = false before the scan, or initialize CrcItr = ElementList.end() and gate on that).

The seek target is the sum of two stream-derived quantities, the size-field position and the size-field length. With a crafted element header these can be large enough that the addition overflows the offset type or points past end-of-file, so the subsequent FindNextElement() begins parsing at a bogus position (reading whatever is there, or failing in ways the caller does not expect). The impact is bounded by the I/O layer's own EOF handling, but the code performs no validation that GetSizePosition() + GetSizeLength() is within the stream. The implementation should verify the computed start offset lies within the parent's bounds before seeking (CWE-190 leading to out-of-range read).

On loop exit with an outstanding upper element, the current ElementLevelA is published to the caller via FoundElt. ElementLevelA is whatever the last FindNextElement() returned or was last assigned; in the dummy/break and delete paths above the element it once pointed at may already have been freed, and this assignment can therefore hand the caller a dangling pointer that the ancestor level will dereference (use-after-free, CWE-416). The implementation must guarantee that the element published through FoundElt is still live and owned appropriately at this point; the coupling between the delete paths and this hand-back is fragile and should be confirmed.

summary: This is libebml's EbmlMaster::Read, a recursive EBML/Matroska container parser operating directly on untrusted file/network data. The dominant flaw is an unsigned 64-bit underflow at line 30 (GetEndPosition() - ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition()): a child element that claims to end past its parent inflates the byte budget MaxSizeToRead to ~2^64, defeating the loop's zero-only termination tests and causing unbounded reads/allocations and denial of service (the libebml EbmlMaster underflow, CVE-2015-8792). Uncontrolled recursion at line 40 gives an independent stack-exhaustion DoS, and the CRC cleanup deletes through a possibly-stale iterator when the bChecksumUsed member is not scoped to the call.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: EbmlMaster::Read(EbmlStream&, const EbmlSemanticContext&, int&, EbmlElement*&, bool, ScopeMode) — line 2
reviewed_anchors.reason: Recursive parser over untrusted EBML input; recursion via line 40 has no depth bound.
Pure early-out on a caller-supplied enum; no attacker-controlled data touched.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: delete loop over ElementList with IsLocked() guard — lines 10-15
reviewed_anchors.reason: Frees only unlocked prior children before clearing; standard cleanup, no attacker-controlled length or index beyond vector bounds.
Unsigned budget; its unsignedness is what makes the line-30 underflow and the <=0 guards exploitable.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: IsFiniteSize()/GetSize() budget init and 0x7FFFFFFF default — lines 18-21
reviewed_anchors.reason: Initial budget is either the declared size or a fixed 2GiB cap; the danger is the later recomputation, not this assignment.
Adds two stream-derived offsets with no range check; possible offset overflow / out-of-range seek (CWE-190).
Allocator/parser call in another TU; it decrements MaxSizeToRead by reference but the reviewable bug is the caller-side underflow at line 30.
Unsigned MaxSizeToRead > 0 loop guard cannot detect budget overshoot; contributes to the non-termination after underflow.
Primary bug: unsigned underflow when child end exceeds parent end; wraps budget to ~2^64 (CWE-191).
Frees the dummy and breaks on unknown size; no length copied into a fixed buffer, no residual dangling use within this branch.
Unbounded recursion depth follows attacker-controlled nesting; stack exhaustion (CWE-674).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: DeleteElement / ValueIsSet() / push_back keep-or-free logic — lines 45-61
reviewed_anchors.reason: Ownership bookkeeping is internally consistent (kept => not deleted, not kept => deleted or broken on).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: UpperEltFound>0 unwinding with goto processCrc and ElementLevelA = FoundElt — lines 64-70
reviewed_anchors.reason: Counter unwinding on ints; the risky publish of ElementLevelA is captured by the FoundElt finding at lines 83-84.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: UpperEltFound<0 unwinding and MaxSizeToRead <= 0 finish test — lines 72-79
reviewed_anchors.reason: The unsigned <=0 concern is captured once under the line-28 comparison finding; not repeated per site.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: FoundElt = ElementLevelA on loop exit — lines 83-84
reviewed_anchors.reason: Publishes a possibly-freed element pointer to the caller; potential use-after-free (CWE-416).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: CRC scan: EbmlId compare, Checksum copy, CrcItr assignment — lines 89-99
reviewed_anchors.reason: CrcItr left uninitialized when no CRC found; paired with un-reset bChecksumUsed it enables an invalid delete.
Deletes through CrcItr gated only by a member flag not reset per call; stale/uninitialized-iterator delete (CWE-457/824).
Sets a status flag with no attacker-controlled data or memory operation.

findings.title: Unsigned 64-bit underflow of read budget when child end exceeds parent end
findings.code:
if (IsFiniteSize() && ElementLevelA->IsFiniteSize())
        MaxSizeToRead = GetEndPosition() - ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition(); // even if it's the default value
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow of unsigned size (unbounded read / DoS)
findings.invariant: The child element must end at or before the parent master's end: ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition() <= GetEndPosition(). The overshoot case must be rejected (or MaxSizeToRead clamped to 0) BEFORE the subtraction.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted EBML/Matroska file declares a child whose size places its end position beyond the parent's end. GetEndPosition() - ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition() underflows in uint64 to a value near 2^64, so MaxSizeToRead becomes effectively unlimited. The loop guard (MaxSizeToRead > 0) and the finish tests (MaxSizeToRead <= 0) never trip on size, and the parser keeps fetching/allocating elements far past the parent's bounds — reading foreign/adjacent stream data, doing unbounded work, and denying service.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no guard exists between line 29 and line 30 rejecting child_end > parent_end, and that GetEndPosition() values are attacker-controlled and unsigned so the subtraction wraps rather than saturating.

findings.title: Uncontrolled recursion on nested master elements (stack exhaustion)
findings.anchor: ElementLevelA->Read(inDataStream, EBML_CONTEXT(ElementLevelA), UpperEltFound, FoundElt, AllowDummyElt, ReadFully);
findings.code: ElementLevelA->Read(inDataStream, EBML_CONTEXT(ElementLevelA), UpperEltFound, FoundElt, AllowDummyElt, ReadFully);
findings.invariant: EBML nesting depth must be bounded to a fixed maximum before recursing, or the traversal must be iterative, so attacker-controlled depth cannot exhaust the call stack.
findings.failure_mode: Each nested master element only needs a few header bytes, so an attacker can nest masters to arbitrary depth. Every level adds a Read() stack frame; a deeply nested file overflows the process stack and crashes before MaxSizeToRead is exhausted — a reliable remote/file-based DoS independent of the underflow bug.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the surrounding library imposes any global nesting-depth limit before this recursive call; absent that, confirm depth is attacker-controlled.

findings.title: Unsigned budget comparisons cannot detect overshoot (defeated termination)
findings.invariant: The remaining-budget guard must treat any overshoot (would-be negative remainder) as end-of-level; with an unsigned type only ==0 is detectable, so the code must clamp or check before subtracting.
findings.failure_mode: Because MaxSizeToRead is uint64, this loop guard and the 'MaxSizeToRead <= 0' finish tests at lines 66 and 78 only distinguish zero from non-zero. When the line-30 subtraction overshoots, the intended 'we've consumed past the end, stop' signal is impossible to express, so the loop continues. This turns the bounds error into a non-terminating read/allocate loop rather than a clean stop.
findings.what_to_confirm: That MaxSizeToRead is an unsigned type at all use sites and that no separate signed remaining-bytes accounting exists to catch overshoot.

findings.title: CRC deletion through stale/uninitialized iterator when bChecksumUsed is not reset per call
findings.bug_class: Use of uninitialized/stale iterator (invalid free / memory corruption)
findings.invariant: The delete must run only when THIS call located a CRC child; either reset bChecksumUsed = false before the scan, or initialize CrcItr = ElementList.end() and gate the delete on CrcItr != end().
findings.failure_mode: bChecksumUsed is a class member not reset here, and CrcItr is uninitialized unless a CRC child is found. If a prior Read() set bChecksumUsed = true and a later Read() of the same object finds no CRC child, 'delete *CrcItr; Remove(CrcItr);' runs on a stale/garbage iterator — dereferencing invalid memory, deleting an arbitrary pointer, or corrupting the container.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether bChecksumUsed is reset to false anywhere before this scan and whether EbmlMaster objects are re-Read; if not, the stale-iterator delete is reachable.

findings.title: Out-of-range seek from unchecked offset addition
findings.invariant: GetSizePosition() + GetSizeLength() must be verified to lie within the parent's bounds (and not overflow the offset type) before seeking.
findings.failure_mode: Both operands come from an attacker-controlled element header. A crafted header can make the sum overflow or point past EOF, so the following FindNextElement() starts parsing at a bogus position. Impact depends on the I/O layer's EOF handling, but no bounds check is performed here.
findings.what_to_confirm: The width/signedness of the file-position type and whether the I/O layer rejects out-of-range seeks.

findings.title: Possible dangling element published to caller via FoundElt
findings.invariant: The element published through FoundElt must be a live, owned object at the point of assignment; any path that freed ElementLevelA must not reach this hand-back.
findings.failure_mode: ElementLevelA is the last value produced by FindNextElement() or reassigned in the loop; several branches above delete the element they processed. On loop exit with UpperEltFound > 0, assigning that pointer to the caller's FoundElt can hand out a freed pointer that the ancestor level later dereferences, causing a use-after-free.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace every loop-exit path with UpperEltFound > 0 and confirm ElementLevelA still points to a live element (not one freed in the dummy/break or DeleteElement paths).

audit_checklist: Confirm there is no guard rejecting ElementLevelA->GetEndPosition() > GetEndPosition() before the line-30 subtraction; add 'reject/clamp if child end exceeds parent end' to prevent the uint64 underflow.
Verify MaxSizeToRead and all GetEndPosition()/GetSize() values are unsigned and attacker-controlled, so the subtraction wraps to ~2^64 rather than a small value.
Check for any maximum EBML nesting-depth limit before the recursive ElementLevelA->Read() at line 40; if absent, deeply nested files cause stack exhaustion.
Confirm the loop's 'MaxSizeToRead > 0' and the 'MaxSizeToRead <= 0' finish tests can never detect overshoot given the unsigned type, and that no separate signed remaining-bytes accounting exists.
Verify bChecksumUsed is reset to false (or CrcItr initialized to end() and checked) before the CRC scan, so a re-Read with no CRC child cannot delete through a stale iterator.
Validate GetSizePosition() + GetSizeLength() cannot overflow and lands within the parent bounds before setFilePointer at line 26.
Trace all loop-exit paths that set FoundElt = ElementLevelA (line 84) and confirm the published element was not already freed in the dummy/break or DeleteElement branches.
